Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Bernard Baars

b. 1946

Global Workspace Theory: consciousness as what gets broadcast across the brain.

Bernard Baars’s theory of consciousness is best understood as an empirical, cognitive-neuroscientific functionalism. Its central claim is not merely that consciousness “correlates” with neural activity, nor that consciousness is an ineffable inner glow added to cognition. Rather, Baars argues that consciousness is a biologically evolved, limited-capacity, globally integrative mode of information processing. Conscious contents become available to many otherwise specialized, largely unconscious systems: memory, attention, learning, action selection, emotional appraisal, self-monitoring, language, and executive control. In Baars’s own framing, the puzzle is how a “serial, integrated, and very limited stream of consciousness” can arise from a nervous system that is mostly unconscious, distributed, parallel, and vastly higher-capacity.

This report treats Baars as a major figure in the transition from late twentieth-century cognitive science to contemporary neuroscience of consciousness. His Global Workspace Theory, or GWT, begins in cognitive architecture and artificial-intelligence “blackboard” models, moves through experimental psychology, and later becomes one of the main ancestors of the Global Neuronal Workspace approach associated with Stanislas Dehaene, Jean-Pierre Changeux, and others. The philosophical position that emerges is a form of empirical naturalism: consciousness is real, causally functional, biologically adaptive, scientifically tractable, and deeply dependent on unconscious cognition. It is not, for Baars, a Cartesian substance, a mere epiphenomenon, or an explanatorily primitive mystery sealed off from science.

1. Historical position: Baars after behaviorism, before contemporary consciousness science

Baars’s importance is partly historical. For much of the twentieth century, especially under behaviorism and then under some strands of early cognitive science, consciousness was treated as scientifically suspect. The cognitive revolution restored internal representations, memory, attention, language, and problem-solving, but often left consciousness itself marginal. Baars’s work helped make consciousness experimentally respectable again by proposing that it could be treated as an empirical variable: compare matched conscious and unconscious cases, then ask what functional and neural differences follow.

His Global Workspace Theory was first proposed in the early 1980s and developed systematically in A Cognitive Theory of Consciousness in 1988. Baars later emphasized that GWT emerged during the period in which consciousness was “re-emerging from neglect” in the sciences of mind. The timing matters. Baars is not a classical behaviorist, not a Cartesian introspectionist, and not primarily a metaphysician of qualia. He is a cognitive scientist trying to explain why consciousness exists, what it does, how it can be studied, and how it may be implemented in the brain.

The intellectual ancestry of GWT also matters. Baars draws on artificial-intelligence blackboard architectures: systems in which many specialist processes contribute to and draw from a shared workspace. The lineage includes Selfridge’s “pandemonium” model, Newell’s blackboard architecture, and systems such as Hearsay for speech recognition. Baars uses this computational lineage not to reduce consciousness to ordinary computation in a simplistic way, but to model how a distributed system can coordinate specialized processes through a limited shared medium.

In philosophy of mind, Baars occupies an intermediate but distinctive position. He is close to functionalism because consciousness is identified by its role in a cognitive architecture. He is close to cognitive naturalism because he thinks the mind should be studied using empirical psychology and neuroscience rather than armchair metaphysics alone. He is close to some forms of representationalism because conscious experiences are informational contents shaped by unconscious contexts. He is unlike eliminativists because he treats conscious experience as real and scientifically central. He is unlike epiphenomenalists because he insists that consciousness performs indispensable biological and cognitive functions. He is unlike dualists because he does not posit a nonphysical mind-substance. And he is unlike philosophers who sharply separate “access consciousness” from “phenomenal consciousness” as a matter of conceptual analysis, because he thinks that relation must be investigated empirically rather than stipulated in advance.

2. The basic problem: serial consciousness in a parallel brain

The core contrast in Baars is between conscious and unconscious processing. The brain is massively parallel, high-capacity, distributed, and mostly unconscious. Consciousness, by contrast, is serial or quasi-serial, limited in capacity, internally coherent, reportable, and fleeting. This contrast is not an embarrassment for GWT; it is the starting point. Consciousness is limited because it is the system’s means of producing globally coherent access among many competing, specialized, unconscious processes.

Baars’s answer is that conscious contents function like broadcasts. At any given moment, many unconscious processors compete to place information into a global workspace. The winning content becomes conscious and is broadcast widely to other systems. Those receiving systems may include memory, action routines, emotional systems, language, evaluative processes, problem-solving procedures, and self-related interpretive systems. Consciousness is therefore not a separate inner substance. It is a global-access condition in a cognitive-neural architecture.

This is why Baars often speaks of consciousness as a “gateway.” Consciousness allows otherwise separated brain functions to influence each other. A consciously perceived word can recruit visual processing, phonology, semantics, episodic memory, action planning, emotional evaluation, and self-relevant interpretation. An unconscious stimulus may still produce local sensory processing, but it does not mobilize the same broad coalition of cognitive resources.

The philosophical significance is considerable. Baars reverses a common assumption. Instead of asking, “Why does physical processing have an inner glow?” he asks, “What form of access, coordination, and control is made possible when a content becomes conscious?” This does not eliminate metaphysical questions, but it changes the explanatory burden. Consciousness is treated first as a biological-cognitive achievement: a way for a complex organism to bind, prioritize, learn, decide, and act.

3. Method: contrastive analysis and reportability

Baars’s method is one of the most important parts of his contribution. He proposes that consciousness can be studied by contrastive analysis: compare conscious and unconscious versions of closely matched events. For example, compare a visible word with a backward-masked word, attended perception with inattentional blindness, one image in binocular rivalry with the suppressed image, wakefulness with anesthesia or deep sleep, or reportable perception with subliminal perception.

The operational marker is usually accurate reportability. If a subject can reliably report a content, that content is treated as conscious. Baars does not think reportability is metaphysically identical to consciousness in a trivial behaviorist sense. Rather, reportability is an empirical handle: a way of identifying conscious access so that one can compare it with unconscious processing. This is central to Baars’s scientific strategy. Consciousness becomes experimentally tractable because it can be manipulated, contrasted, and correlated with cognitive and neural changes.

This methodological move also has philosophical consequences. Baars does not begin from private incorrigibility or from the alleged impossibility of third-person science. He begins from public contrasts: what changes when the same or similar information is consciously available rather than unconsciously processed? His approach therefore weakens the traditional opposition between first-person phenomenology and third-person neuroscience. First-person report remains indispensable, but it is embedded in experimental design.

4. The Global Workspace architecture

The simplest version of GWT has four broad elements.

First, there are many specialized unconscious processors. These are domain-specific or task-specific systems that perform perception, memory retrieval, syntactic parsing, motor preparation, affective evaluation, and countless other operations. They are powerful, fast, and often automatic, but they are not globally available by default.

Second, there is a limited-capacity workspace. It can hold only a small amount of coherent content at a time. Baars often compares this to the bright spot on a theater stage. The metaphor is useful only if it is not misunderstood. The “stage” is not a Cartesian place where a homunculus watches inner objects. It is a functional architecture for making selected contents available to many systems.

Third, attention controls access to the workspace. Attention is like a spotlight that selects, amplifies, or stabilizes certain contents. However, attention and consciousness are not simply identical. Attention can shape what becomes conscious, and conscious contents can in turn recruit attention. Baars’s theory therefore allows a two-way relation: attention enables access to consciousness, while conscious events guide future attentional selection.

Fourth, conscious contents are broadcast. This is the heart of the theory. Once a content becomes conscious, it is distributed to many unconscious processors, which can use it for interpretation, learning, action, memory encoding, decision-making, and self-monitoring. This is why consciousness is limited but powerful. Its capacity is narrow, but its reach is wide.

Baars’s theater metaphor includes three important components: the stage of working memory, the attentional spotlight, and the “audience” of unconscious systems. Contexts, expectations, goals, and self-related frames operate largely “backstage,” shaping what appears in consciousness without themselves necessarily becoming conscious. This is one of Baars’s most philosophically fertile ideas: consciousness is not the whole mind, but the visible phase of an overwhelmingly unconscious cognitive ecology.

5. Conscious and unconscious cognition

Baars’s theory requires a strong unconscious. The unconscious is not merely repressed material in the psychoanalytic sense, nor merely subthreshold sensory input. It is the vast infrastructure of cognition. Unconscious systems parse speech, identify objects, generate expectations, guide attention, retrieve memories, initiate action tendencies, shape affective reactions, and maintain self-contexts. Consciousness depends on them at every moment.

The conscious/unconscious distinction can be summarized this way. Conscious processing is limited, coherent, serial or quasi-serial, reportable, globally available, and especially important for novelty, conflict, decision, learning, and voluntary control. Unconscious processing is high-capacity, parallel, specialized, automatic, efficient, and often local rather than globally broadcast. Baars’s point is not that consciousness is “smarter” than the unconscious. It is that consciousness allows separately intelligent unconscious systems to coordinate.

This gives Baars a distinctive answer to the old question of why consciousness is needed. If every cognitive operation could be handled by local automatic routines, consciousness would be unnecessary. But organisms encounter novelty, ambiguity, conflict, error, and shifting goals. In those conditions, a system needs a way to bring information into a shared arena where many resources can be recruited. Consciousness is that shared arena.

6. Neural implementation: from cognitive workspace to cortico-thalamic dynamics

Baars’s earliest theory is architectural and cognitive, but his later work increasingly links GWT to neuroscience. The most general neural claim is that conscious contents involve large-scale cortico-thalamic activity rather than activity in one small “consciousness center.” Baars and colleagues describe the global workspace as a functional hub rather than a single anatomical hub. Conscious contents may arise in different cortico-thalamic regions depending on modality and task, then propagate widely through recurrent connections.

This is important: Baars does not reduce consciousness to the frontal cortex alone, the thalamus alone, the visual cortex alone, or any single neural site. The workspace is dynamic. It involves binding and propagation: a content is locally stabilized or bound, then broadcast through long-range cortico-cortical and cortico-thalamic pathways. Baars’s later dynamic version of GWT emphasizes winner-take-all coalitions, recurrent activity, and brief conscious moments on the order of roughly 100–200 milliseconds.

Sensory consciousness is typically tied to the corresponding sensory projection areas. Visual experience, for example, depends on visual cortical systems; auditory experience depends on auditory systems. But for a stimulus to become conscious in the full global-workspace sense, local sensory activation is not enough. Conscious perception tends to involve wider activation, including frontoparietal and other association areas. Studies of masked words, binocular rivalry, inattentional blindness, anesthesia, sleep, and related contrasts are used to support this distinction between local unconscious processing and globally available conscious content.

Baars also gives the thalamus a major role because thalamocortical organization is well suited to recurrent binding and broadcasting. In the dynamic GWT picture, the cortico-thalamic complex is contrasted with systems such as the cerebellum: both may be computationally powerful, but the cortico-thalamic system has the right global-access architecture for conscious contents.

This neural theory is not presented as complete. Baars acknowledges that multiple proposed mechanisms may contribute to conscious access, including frontocortical ignition, dynamic core activity, synchrony, and thalamocortical distribution. His position is that these mechanisms may not be mutually exclusive; they may describe different levels or aspects of the same global-access process.

7. The functions of consciousness

Baars is unusually explicit about the functions of consciousness. His theory is not merely a theory of neural correlates. It is a theory of what consciousness does.

The most general function is global access. Consciousness makes a selected content available to a wide range of otherwise separate systems. If one had to name Baars’s single most important claim, it would be this: consciousness exists to enable widespread access, integration, and coordination in a distributed cognitive system.

From this central function follow several more specific functions.

Consciousness supports definition and context-setting. A conscious event helps define “what situation we are in” by integrating sensory input with conceptual, goal-related, emotional, and self-related contexts. The same physical stimulus can mean different things depending on context; consciousness helps establish the currently relevant frame.

Consciousness supports novel learning and adaptation. Baars repeatedly argues that significant new learning is typically evoked by conscious contents, even when the later learning process or its outcome becomes unconscious. Consciousness is especially important when automatic routines are insufficient: new tasks, surprising events, errors, ambiguous stimuli, and unfamiliar goals.

Consciousness supports working memory. Conscious inner speech, imagery, and focal perceptual contents can recruit and coordinate unconscious systems for rehearsal, problem-solving, and temporary maintenance. Baars does not reduce working memory to consciousness, but he treats conscious contents as central to many working-memory operations.

Consciousness supports voluntary control. A conscious goal image, instruction, or intention can recruit unconscious motor and cognitive routines. Voluntary action is not produced by a ghostly will outside the causal order; it is produced when conscious goal representations mobilize lower-level systems. Baars uses examples such as biofeedback and learned control to argue that conscious feedback can bring normally inaccessible processes under some degree of control.

Consciousness supports decision-making and executive competition. Conscious contents provide a domain in which goals, interpretations, and action options can compete. A decision is not merely a private mental act; it is a global selection process in which some goal or representation wins access and guides further processing.

Consciousness supports error detection, editing, and debugging. When an automatic routine fails, consciousness can mark the failure and recruit alternative resources. This is one reason conscious attention is drawn to conflict, novelty, interruption, surprise, and difficulty.

Consciousness supports analogy and creative recombination. Because conscious contents can be made available to multiple knowledge sources, they can be interpreted from different angles. This allows analogical transfer: one domain can be brought to bear on another.

Consciousness supports metacognition and self-monitoring. We can become conscious not only of objects but also of our own uncertainty, effort, remembering, intending, imagining, and knowing. Baars’s later work includes “fringe” experiences such as feelings of knowing and tip-of-the-tongue states, which are not fully explicit conceptual contents but still guide cognition.

Finally, consciousness supports what Baars calls autoprogramming or self-maintenance. Through conscious rehearsal, reflection, planning, and goal-setting, organisms can alter their own future dispositions. A person can set a goal, rehearse a skill, reinterpret a memory, or deliberately attend to a neglected domain. This is one of the places where Baars’s theory has philosophical depth: consciousness is not merely a window on the world; it is a means by which the cognitive system modifies itself.

8. The “observing self” and the self as context

Baars’s theory of the self is not a Cartesian ego theory. The self is not an immaterial observer sitting in the theater. Yet Baars does think that something like an “observing self” has scientific legitimacy if properly naturalized. Conscious contents are not merely broadcast into a void; they are interpreted, evaluated, and organized relative to self-systems. These include systems for first-person perspective, autobiographical continuity, executive control, personality, goal structure, and self-relevant interpretation.

Baars and colleagues associate aspects of this observing self with parietal and prefrontal systems. Parietal regions are linked to perspective and spatial embodiment, while prefrontal regions are linked to executive interpretation, selection, inhibition, and control. Damage to prefrontal systems can alter personality, inhibition, judgment, and the organization of conscious thought, which supports the idea that selfhood is a functional-neural organization rather than a metaphysical primitive.

This view also informs Baars’s treatment of unconscious states. Deep sleep, anesthesia, coma, vegetative states, and some epileptic states are not merely cases in which sensory processing stops. Often some local processing can continue. What is lost is the global availability of contents to self-related interpretive and executive systems. Baars’s suggestive phrase is that in such states the observing subject may not be “at home.”

The philosophical result is a layered theory of selfhood. At the surface are conscious self-reports: “I see,” “I intend,” “I remember.” Beneath those are unconscious self-contexts: expectations, emotional dispositions, autobiographical frames, body schemas, goals, and executive policies. The self is neither an illusion nor a simple object. It is a hierarchical context-system that frames conscious access.

9. Phenomenal consciousness, access consciousness, and the hard problem

Baars’s relation to the access/phenomenal consciousness debate is central for extracting a philosophy of mind from his work. Ned Block famously distinguished phenomenal consciousness, roughly experience or “what it is like,” from access consciousness, meaning availability for reasoning, report, and action. Baars resisted treating that distinction as something settled by conceptual analysis. In a direct response to Block, Baars argued that the relation between personal consciousness and global access is an empirical question, not something that can be decided pretheoretically.

This places Baars on the access side of the philosophical map, but not in a crude way. He does not say merely that consciousness is verbal report. Nor does he deny experience. Instead, he argues that the phenomena we call conscious experience are precisely those that become globally available in the cognitive system. Phenomenology is not discarded; it is operationalized and explained through access, integration, and broadcast.

This is why Baars’s position differs from Chalmers-style hard-problem philosophy. Baars does not begin with the conceivability of zombies or the alleged explanatory gap between physical structure and subjective feel. He begins with experimentally tractable contrasts between conscious and unconscious processing. The traditional mind-body problem, for Baars, loses much of its force when consciousness is treated as a functional-biological process open to empirical investigation. His published work explicitly frames some traditional mind-body worries as scientifically untestable or less relevant to the empirical project.

A fair philosophical reconstruction would say this: Baars is not offering a full metaphysical dissolution of the hard problem. He is offering a research program that explains a large part of what consciousness does, how it differs from unconscious processing, and why it matters biologically. Whether that fully explains phenomenal “what-it-is-like” character remains contested. Critics from the phenomenal-consciousness tradition can argue that global availability explains access, report, and control, but not subjective feel. Baars’s likely reply is that such alleged residue should not be assumed in advance; it should be investigated through the same contrastive, empirical methods.

10. Baars’s anti-epiphenomenalism

Baars is strongly anti-epiphenomenalist. If consciousness has the functions he attributes to it, then it cannot be a causally inert byproduct. Conscious events recruit unconscious resources, guide learning, shape voluntary action, enable report, support self-monitoring, and help resolve novelty and conflict. Baars explicitly argues that if consciousness is lost through habituation, automaticity, distraction, masking, anesthesia, or similar conditions, then the functions associated with consciousness are inhibited or destroyed.

This matters philosophically because epiphenomenalism has often appealed to the thought that physical processes do all the causal work while consciousness merely accompanies them. Baars rejects that framing by redescribing consciousness itself as a class of physical-functional processes: global broadcasting, access, and integration. Consciousness is causally efficacious not because it floats above neural processes, but because the neural-cognitive condition of global availability has downstream effects.

In this sense Baars’s anti-epiphenomenalism is not a separate metaphysical doctrine. It follows from the architecture. If a conscious content is broadcast to many systems, and those systems use it for learning, decision, memory, and action, then consciousness is causally relevant.

11. Artificial consciousness in Baars: functional, not necessarily phenomenal

Artificial consciousness is not incidental to Baars’s theory, but it is treated carefully. Because GWT has roots in AI blackboard architectures, it naturally raises the question of whether an artificial system could implement a global workspace. Baars and collaborators, especially Stan Franklin, developed IDA and later LIDA as computational architectures inspired by GWT. IDA was designed for a practical U.S. Navy personnel-assignment task, and Baars describes it as an existence proof that a global-workspace-like architecture can perform useful functions in an artificial agent.

The crucial qualification is that Baars and Franklin distinguish functional consciousness from phenomenal consciousness. They define a “functionally conscious” agent as an autonomous agent that implements GWT-like mechanisms to perform real-world tasks. But they explicitly do not claim that IDA is phenomenally conscious in the sense of having subjective experience. They also state that necessary and sufficient conditions for conscious cognition in the brain are not yet known.

This gives Baars a cautious but meaningful position on artificial consciousness. Artificial systems can, in principle, instantiate functional consciousness if they have a limited-capacity global workspace, competition for access, broadcast to specialized processors, learning, action selection, and self-monitoring. But whether such systems have subjective experience is not settled merely by functional analogy. Baars’s position is therefore more conservative than strong AI claims that any suitable computation automatically has experience, but more open than biological chauvinism that rules out machine consciousness a priori.

LIDA deepens the functional picture. It models cognition as cycles in which contents compete for consciousness, a winning coalition is broadcast, learning is updated, and action selection proceeds. In this framework, attention and learning are tightly linked: conscious access helps update the system and prepare future action.

The implied philosophy of artificial mind is this: consciousness is architecturally possible in artificial systems at the functional level, but phenomenal consciousness remains an empirical and theoretical question. Baars does not license careless claims that present-day machines are conscious merely because they process language or behave intelligently. A genuine Baarsian artificial consciousness would need a robust global workspace embedded in autonomous perception, action, learning, self-monitoring, and world-directed agency.

12. Extracting Baars’s philosophy of mind

Baars does not present himself primarily as a systematic philosopher, but a systematic philosophy of mind can be extracted from his work. It has the following core theses.

First, the mind is a layered biological information-processing system. Consciousness is only one layer. Most mental activity is unconscious, but unconscious processing is not nonmental; it is the infrastructure of mind. Perception, memory, intention, attention, emotion, and selfhood all involve interactions between unconscious contexts and conscious contents.

Second, consciousness is global availability. A content is conscious when it enters a limited-capacity workspace and becomes available to many specialized systems. This makes Baars a global-access theorist. Consciousness is not identical with local sensory activation, isolated representation, or mere information processing. It is a system-level access condition.

Third, consciousness is functional and adaptive. Its biological role is to coordinate a distributed organism under conditions of novelty, uncertainty, conflict, and goal-directed action. This makes Baars a functionalist, but not a purely armchair functionalist. The relevant functional roles must be tied to empirical cognitive science and neuroscience.

Fourth, consciousness is causally efficacious. Conscious contents guide attention, recruit resources, enable learning, influence action, and shape self-monitoring. Baars’s view is therefore incompatible with epiphenomenalism.

Fifth, the self is a contextual-executive organization. The self is not a Cartesian witness outside the causal order. It is a set of systems that frame, interpret, evaluate, and control conscious contents. Selfhood is real, but it is functionally and neurally distributed.

Sixth, phenomenology is scientifically approachable. Baars does not eliminate subjective experience. He treats it as something that can be studied through report, contrastive analysis, and neural-cognitive modeling. He resists the idea that “phenomenal consciousness” is conceptually insulated from global access.

Seventh, the mind-body problem should be naturalized. Baars’s approach does not try to solve the mind-body problem by metaphysical declaration. It changes the terrain: ask what consciousness does, how conscious and unconscious processes differ, what neural mechanisms support global access, and how conscious contents affect learning and action. Traditional metaphysical puzzles are not ignored, but they are subordinated to empirical theory.

Eighth, artificial consciousness is possible in a functional sense. A machine could implement global-workspace functions, and Baars’s own collaborations with Franklin explore that possibility. But phenomenal machine consciousness is not asserted merely on the basis of implementing a cognitive architecture.

A concise formulation of the extracted philosophy would be: consciousness is the biologically implemented, limited-capacity, reportable, globally broadcast phase of a mostly unconscious cognitive architecture; its contents are selected by attention, shaped by contexts, made available to many specialist systems, and used for learning, voluntary control, self-monitoring, and adaptive action.

13. Relation to major philosophical positions

Baars’s view resembles functionalism because mental states are understood by their causal roles within a system. But it is more specific than generic functionalism. It does not say merely that pain, perception, or belief are whatever plays the right role. It gives a detailed architectural role for consciousness: global broadcast from a limited workspace to distributed processors.

It resembles biological naturalism because consciousness is treated as an evolved biological function implemented by the brain. But Baars is less metaphysically focused than John Searle and more computationally open. He does not reduce consciousness to syntax or symbol manipulation, but he does think computational architectures can illuminate the functional structure of consciousness.

It resembles Dennettian anti-Cartesianism because it rejects a privileged inner theater with a homuncular observer. Yet Baars uses a theater metaphor positively, provided it is understood functionally rather than literally. This creates a subtle contrast: Dennett warned against the “Cartesian theater,” while Baars argues that a non-Cartesian theater metaphor can capture real features of conscious access.

It conflicts with strict epiphenomenalism because consciousness does real work. It conflicts with strong dualism because consciousness is treated as part of the natural causal order. It conflicts with eliminativism because conscious experience is not dismissed. And it conflicts with any view that treats introspection alone as the foundation of consciousness science.

Its most important philosophical opponent is probably the access/phenomenal distinction as used by Block and later philosophers. Block defines phenomenal consciousness as experience and access consciousness as availability for reasoning, report, and action. Baars’s theory strongly explains access; the dispute is whether that also explains phenomenality. Baars’s own answer is that the separation should not be assumed before the science is done.

14. Strengths of Baars’s approach

The first strength is explanatory breadth. GWT connects perception, attention, working memory, learning, voluntary action, metacognition, selfhood, and unconscious processing within one architecture. It does not treat consciousness as an isolated phenomenon.

The second strength is empirical tractability. Contrastive analysis gives researchers a method for comparing conscious and unconscious processing. This has influenced later work on masking, attentional blink, binocular rivalry, anesthesia, sleep, and reportability.

The third strength is its account of limited capacity. Many theories treat the narrowness of consciousness as a defect. Baars turns it into a functional necessity. A global broadcast must be coherent enough to be usable; if too many incompatible contents were globally broadcast at once, coordination would fail. Limited capacity is the price of global access.

The fourth strength is its treatment of the unconscious. Baars avoids both Cartesian overvaluation of consciousness and reductive dismissal of it. Consciousness is powerful because the unconscious is powerful. The conscious mind is not the whole mind; it is the coordination interface of the mind.

The fifth strength is its bridge between cognitive science and neuroscience. GWT began as a cognitive architecture, but later versions connect it to cortico-thalamic dynamics, long-range connectivity, frontoparietal activation, sensory projection areas, and recurrent broadcasting.

15. Limitations and pressure points

The first limitation is the hard-problem objection. A critic can grant that GWT explains report, access, attention, learning, and control, while denying that it explains why any of this should feel like something. Baars’s response is methodological: do not assume a gap that science cannot close; investigate the relation between phenomenology and access empirically. Still, for philosophers committed to a strong phenomenal/access distinction, Baars may seem to explain the functional profile of consciousness rather than phenomenal character itself.

The second limitation is the reportability problem. Because Baars uses accurate report as a major operational marker, critics may worry that the theory identifies consciousness too closely with what can be reported. Some conscious experiences may be difficult to report, and some neural activity associated with report preparation may be mistaken for neural activity constitutive of consciousness. Contemporary debates over frontoparietal activity often turn on this issue: is such activity part of consciousness itself, or part of task performance, decision, and report? The Global Neuronal Workspace tradition itself recognizes that accessibility, access, and conscious access need careful distinction.

The third limitation is neural specificity. Baars’s broad cortico-thalamic and frontoparietal claims are plausible as a research program, but the exact neural mechanisms remain disputed. Baars himself notes that multiple mechanisms—frontocortical ignition, dynamic core processes, synchrony, thalamic hub dynamics, and thalamocortical broadcasting—may contribute.

The fourth limitation is the richness or overflow problem. Some philosophers and psychologists argue that phenomenal experience may be richer than cognitive access or report. If so, global access may be sufficient for reportable consciousness but not necessary for all phenomenal consciousness. Baars’s framework has resources for reply, especially by appealing to partial access, fringe consciousness, and unconscious contexts, but the dispute remains.

The fifth limitation concerns artificial consciousness. Baars’s functional criterion is useful, but it leaves open the phenomenal question. A GWT-style machine might behave as if it has conscious access, perform global broadcasting, and use contents for learning and control. Whether that entails experience depends on further assumptions about functionalism, biology, embodiment, and neural implementation. Baars and Franklin explicitly avoid claiming phenomenal consciousness for IDA, which is philosophically prudent but leaves the deepest AI-consciousness question unresolved.

16. Final synthesis

Baars’s theory should be read as one of the foundational attempts to turn consciousness into a rigorous object of cognitive science. Its central insight is that consciousness is not the whole mind and not an inert accompaniment of the mind. It is a limited but globally powerful mode of access within a mostly unconscious system.

The philosophy of mind extractable from Baars is therefore a global-workspace naturalism. It says that minds are distributed biological architectures; that unconscious cognition is primary in scale and efficiency; that consciousness is a special access-and-broadcast condition; that conscious contents have causal power because they coordinate learning, action, memory, attention, and self-monitoring; that the self is a contextual-executive organization rather than a metaphysical spectator; and that artificial systems may achieve functional consciousness if they implement the relevant architecture, though phenomenal artificial consciousness remains unproven.

Baars’s place in the larger development of philosophy of mind is that of a bridge figure. He connects the cognitive revolution, AI architecture, empirical psychology, and neuroscience of consciousness. He gives philosophers a concrete alternative to both Cartesian introspection and eliminative reduction: a theory in which consciousness is real, functional, natural, limited, reportable, and scientifically investigable. His work does not settle every metaphysical dispute about subjective experience, but it substantially reshapes the question. Instead of asking only what consciousness is “in itself,” Baars asks what consciousness does in a brain, why a complex organism would need it, and how its presence changes cognition. That remains one of the most influential and productive strategies in the contemporary science and philosophy of consciousness.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
